Question 1: Does Wage Discrimination Exist in Starting Salaries?

Author

Ha Tien Nguyen

Published

March 11, 2026

Research Question: Does wage discrimination exist in the starting salaries of faculty members (i.e., salaries in the year hired)?

Significance level: α = 0.05 (two-sided tests throughout).

1. Setup

Code 
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

2. Load & Clean Data

Code 
df <- read.table("salary.txt", header = TRUE, strip.white = TRUE)
df$sex   <- factor(df$sex,   levels = c("M", "F"))
df$field <- factor(df$field)

cat("After startyr == year filter:", nrow(df), "rows\n")
After startyr == year filter: 19792 rows

2.6 Final Dataset

Code 
cat("Final dataset:", nrow(df), "faculty members\n")
Final dataset: 19792 faculty members
Code 
cat("  Female:", sum(df$sex == "F"), " | Male:", sum(df$sex == "M"), "\n")
  Female: 3926  | Male: 15866
Code 
cat("  Hire years:", min(df$startyr), "–", max(df$startyr), "\n")
  Hire years: 48 – 95
Code 
cat("  Salary range: $", min(df$salary), "– $", max(df$salary), "\n")
  Salary range: $ 1200 – $ 14464

3. Descriptive Statistics

3.1 Summary by Sex

Code 
tapply(df$salary, df$sex, function(x) {
  round(c(n = length(x), mean = mean(x), median = median(x),
          sd = sd(x), IQR = IQR(x)), 2)
})
$M
       n     mean   median       sd      IQR 
15866.00  4854.54  4505.00  2064.17  2718.00 

$F
      n    mean  median      sd     IQR 
3926.00 4185.46 3996.00 1523.38 1798.25

3.2 Boxplots by Sex

Code 
ggplot(df, aes(x = sex, y = salary, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(M = "Male", F = "Female")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(M = "#ff7f0e", F = "#1f77b4"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Starting Salary by Sex", x = NULL, y = "Monthly Salary ($)") +
  theme_minimal()

3.3 Histograms by Sex

Code 
ggplot(df, aes(x = salary, fill = sex)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~sex, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y",
             labeller = labeller(sex = c(M = "Male", F = "Female"))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(M = "#ff7f0e", F = "#1f77b4"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Starting Salary Distribution by Sex", x = "Monthly Salary ($)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

3.4 Summary by Field and Sex

Code 
aggregate(salary ~ field + sex, data = df,
          FUN = function(x) round(c(n = length(x), mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x)), 2))
  field sex salary.n salary.mean salary.sd
1  Arts   M  2038.00     3883.72   1478.36
2 Other   M 10408.00     4830.07   2016.85
3  Prof   M  3420.00     5507.56   2257.71
4  Arts   F   802.00     3748.81   1235.44
5 Other   F  2735.00     4177.28   1478.27
6  Prof   F   389.00     5143.19   1900.66
Code 
ggplot(df, aes(x = sex, y = salary, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~field) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c(M = "Male", F = "Female")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(M = "#ff7f0e", F = "#1f77b4"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Starting Salary by Sex and Field", x = NULL, y = "Monthly Salary ($)") +
  theme_minimal()

3.5 Salary by Hire Cohort

Code 
cohort <- df %>%
  group_by(startyr, sex) %>%
  summarise(mean_sal = mean(salary), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(cohort, aes(x = startyr, y = mean_sal, colour = sex)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(M = "#ff7f0e", F = "#1f77b4"),
                      labels = c(M = "Male", F = "Female")) +
  labs(title = "Mean Starting Salary by Hire Year and Sex",
       x = "Year Hired", y = "Mean Monthly Salary ($)", colour = "Sex") +
  theme_minimal()

4. Inferential Analysis

4.1 Welch Two-Sample t-Test

\(H_0: \mu_F = \mu_M\) vs \(H_A: \mu_F \neq \mu_M\)

Code 
t.test(salary ~ sex, data = df, alternative = "two.sided")

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  salary by sex
t = 22.82, df = 7898.2, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group M and group F is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 611.6075 726.5572
sample estimates:
mean in group M mean in group F 
       4854.545        4185.462

4.2 Multiple Linear Regression (Salary Level)

\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1\text{Female}_i + \beta_2\text{Field}_i + \beta_3\text{YrDeg}_i + \beta_4\text{HireYear}_i + \varepsilon_i\]

Code 
ols <- lm(salary ~ sex + field + yrdeg + startyr, data = df)
coeftest(ols, vcov = vcovHC(ols, type = "HC3"))

t test of coefficients:

             Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 4518.7880   117.3163  38.518 < 2.2e-16 ***
sexF        -441.2949    29.6914 -14.863 < 2.2e-16 ***
fieldOther   763.6233    30.7219  24.856 < 2.2e-16 ***
fieldProf   1522.6167    44.1703  34.471 < 2.2e-16 ***
yrdeg        -94.1490     3.0152 -31.224 < 2.2e-16 ***
startyr       82.5430     3.0181  27.350 < 2.2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Code 
summary(ols)$r.squared
[1] 0.1274439

4.3 Multiple Linear Regression (Log Salary)

Using log(salary) to interpret the sex gap as a percentage difference and to address right skew.

Code 
log_ols <- lm(log(salary) ~ sex + field + yrdeg + startyr, data = df)
coeftest(log_ols, vcov = vcovHC(log_ols, type = "HC3"))

t test of coefficients:

               Estimate  Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  8.18125945  0.02422160 337.767 < 2.2e-16 ***
sexF        -0.09031574  0.00696594 -12.965 < 2.2e-16 ***
fieldOther   0.16708878  0.00768411  21.745 < 2.2e-16 ***
fieldProf    0.30859983  0.00978688  31.532 < 2.2e-16 ***
yrdeg       -0.01747859  0.00053547 -32.642 < 2.2e-16 ***
startyr      0.01709527  0.00053126  32.179 < 2.2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Code 
summary(log_ols)$r.squared
[1] 0.1122202

The sexF coefficient from the log model gives the approximate percentage salary difference for women relative to men: (exp(coef) - 1) * 100.

5. Diagnostics

Code 
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(log_ols)
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
